messages.content:
帮我缩减以下英文学术论文：In this work, we propose the SWDNet method for detecting
stealth web shells based on the triplet network. Firstly,
we constructed a high-quality dataset consisting of three
categories: small web shell, benign files and stealth web shells.
The dataset was pre-processed by converting all files to opcode
and removing duplicates. Then, the files were vectorized using
opcode indexing vectorization method and fed into the triplet
network model for training. The aim of the model was to
make the stealth web shells closer to small web shell and
farther away from benign files, so that their characteristics
were similar to those of small web shell, thus enabling accurate
detection of stealth web shells. Furthermore, we trained a
TextCNN model using the same dataset as a comparison
method and also compared our results with popular tools.
Our experiment results show that SWDNet achieved high
accuracy and F1-score, demonstrating significant improvement
in detecting stealth web shells. However, the size of the
dataset had an impact on our model’s performance during
training. Currently, due to the limited small web shell available
after conversion to opcode and removal of duplicates, our
constructed dataset is constrained. Therefore, in future work,
we will continue to look for ways to construct more extensive
datasets. We also plan to apply our method to more open-
source websites to test the security of the entire software
supply chain process from development to deployment.

In this study, we introduce the SWDNet method for detecting stealth web shells using the triplet network. Our approach involves three main steps. Firstly, we compile a high-quality dataset consisting of small web shells, benign files, and stealth web shells. The dataset is pre-processed by converting all files to opcode and eliminating duplicates. Next, we vectorize the files using the opcode indexing vectorization method and train the triplet network model. The objective of the model is to make the stealth web shells similar to small web shells and dissimilar to benign files, thereby enabling accurate detection of stealth web shells. Additionally, we also train a TextCNN model using the same dataset as a comparison method and compare our results with popular tools.

Our experimental findings demonstrate that SWDNet achieves high accuracy and F1-score, representing a significant improvement in the detection of stealth web shells. However, we encountered challenges during training due to the limited availability of small web shells after opcode conversion and duplicate removal. As a result, the size of our dataset had an impact on the performance of our model. In future work, we will continue to explore methods for building more comprehensive datasets. Additionally, we plan to apply our method to a wider range of open-source websites to evaluate the security of the software supply chain process, encompassing development and deployment stages.

帮我缩减以下英文学术论文：Our approach to identifying stealth web shells involves constructing three categories of sample data: small web shells, benign PHP files, and stealth web shells. We start by collecting small web shells and normal PHP files from public platforms. The collected dataset undergoes several processing steps, including opcode conversion, deduplication, and manual analysis, to create a high-quality dataset. We then insert the small web shells into the normal files according to the following standard to create stealth web shells. These files also undergo the same processing steps. As a result, we obtain three datasets of equal size (583), and each data in the datasets is assigned a unique identifier.

To identify stealth web shells, we construct three categories of sample data: small web shells, benign PHP files, and stealth web shells. Initially, we collect small web shells and normal PHP files from public platforms. The dataset is then subjected to several processing steps, such as opcode conversion, deduplication, and manual analysis, to ensure high quality. In order to create stealth web shells, the small web shells are inserted into the normal files according to a specified standard. These files also undergo the same processing steps. Ultimately, we obtain three datasets, each containing 583 entries, with each data assigned a unique identifier.

帮我缩减以下英文学术论文：
To collect small web shells, we primarily gathered data from the globally popular source code sharing platform, Github. We attempted to gather as many PHP web shell datasets as possible (e.g. \cite{PHP-Webshell-Dataset,JohnTroony-php-webshells} and more). Then, we employed a comprehensive approach based on file size and line count to determine the boundary for the size of small web shells. This was done to ensure that the web shells were not too large or had excessive line numbers, in order to achieve better concealment. Through comparisons, we narrowed down the range of small web shell sizes to be within 8KB. After excluding shells with identical names and sizes, we collected a total of 1816 small web shells.

Next, all the small web shells were converted into opcode sequences, which allowed for more thorough deduplication. Different small web shells may yield the same opcode sequence after compilation, or due to the presence of multiple versions of the same shells as well as various forms of obfuscation, resulting in varying quality of the collected small web shells. Since our model is trained based on opcodes as the fundamental feature, deduplicating the opcode sequences can improve the quality of the model dataset. During the opcode conversion process, three errors occurred that could not be converted due to UnicodeDecodeError. After deduplicating the opcode sequences of the 1813 small web shells, we obtained a total of 701 unique small web shells.

Finally, we carried out another round of manual analysis and judgment. We removed benign shells that were beyond the scope of our research, such as phpinfo(). Additionally, we screened and eliminated small web shells containing '$<$html$>$' tags, as they were not required for our study, as we focused on small web shells that exist in an independent form. In the end, we acquired 583 web shells, with most of them concentrated in the 1KB and 2KB range. Several reached a maximum size of 8KB, but their line numbers were not as extensive. The distribution of small web shell sample sizes is illustrated in Fig1.

To collect small web shells, we primarily sourced data from Github, a globally popular source code sharing platform. We gathered various PHP web shell datasets, such as \cite{PHP-Webshell-Dataset,JohnTroony-php-webshells}, to maximize our collection. To ensure better concealment, we applied a comprehensive approach based on file size and line count to define the boundary for small web shell size. Through comparisons, we determined the size range of small web shells to be within 8KB. After excluding shells with identical names and sizes, we obtained a total of 1816 small web shells.

Next, we converted all small web shells into opcode sequences to facilitate thorough deduplication. Conversion to opcodes allowed us to identify and eliminate duplicates that may arise from compilation or variations in shells due to obfuscation. The deduplication process improved the quality of our model dataset. Despite encountering three errors during opcode conversion, we successfully deduplicated the opcode sequences, resulting in 701 unique small web shells.

Lastly, we conducted manual analysis and judgment. We removed benign shells that fell outside the scope of our research, such as phpinfo(). We also excluded small web shells containing '$<$html$>$' tags, as they were not relevant to our study, which focused on independent small web shells. Ultimately, we obtained 583 web shells, with the majority falling within the 1KB and 2KB range. Some reached a maximum size of 8KB, yet their line numbers were not extensive. The distribution of small web shell sample sizes can be seen in Figure 1.

帮我缩减以下英文学术论文：
The benign PHP files are also mainly collected from Github. For benign PHP files, there are many open-source CMS frameworks available, such as WordPress, Joomla, CodeIgniter, Laravel, Yii, and so on. When it comes to collecting benign files, various factors are taken into account, including the number of stars, project maintenance activity, community participation (Pull Requests, Issues, and Discussions), etc., as standards for ensuring the reliability and quality of the source code. 

In addition, two points should be followed: first, it’s important to collect PHP files from a diverse range of websites. This ensures that the collected files reflect real-world scenarios and are not limited to specific contexts or applications. Second, it is imperative to avoid gathering PHP files that are too small, as they may not be suitable for insertion by small web shell. 

Benign files have a low duplication rate, but they should still be transformed into opcode sequences and deduplicated to ensure that the code quality and smoothly execution without errors.

We collect benign PHP files primarily from Github, focusing on open-source CMS frameworks like WordPress, Joomla, CodeIgniter, Laravel, Yii, among others. When gathering these benign files, we consider various factors, such as the number of stars, project maintenance activity, and community participation (Pull Requests, Issues, and Discussions) to ensure the reliability and quality of the source code. 

To ensure the diversity of the collected files and their applicability to real-world scenarios, it is important to gather PHP files from a range of websites, avoiding limiting the collection to specific contexts or applications. Additionally, we avoid gathering PHP files that are too small, as they may not be suitable for insertion by small web shells. 

Although benign files have a low duplication rate, we still convert them into opcode sequences and deduplicate them to ensure code quality and smooth execution without errors.
帮我翻译并润色为英文学术论文风格：我们首先获取了583个小马，然后与之对应的生成583个良性文件，最后按照stealth web shell的生成标准生成583个stealth web shells.

We initially obtained 583 small web shells, and subsequently generated 583 corresponding benign files. Finally, we followed the generation criteria for stealth web shells to produce 583 stealth web shells.

帮我精简以下英文学术论文：In light of the fact that the number of samples obtained from small web shells decreased significantly after being converted into opcode sequences and further deduplicated, we expanded our dataset to investigate the impact of dataset size on experimental results. To this end, we duplicated the data from small web shells, sequentially numbered them up to 1166, and constructed additional 583 benign files and 583 stealth web shells corresponding to the small web shells. These two sets of data were constructed according to the standard construction for benign files and stealth web shells in Chapter \ref{chapter3}, which involved searching for new data, opcode conversion, deduplication, manual analysis, and numbering them. This resulted in three sets of experimental data, each containing 1166 samples. The datasets were then split into training and test sets with an 8:2 ratio and used to respectively train SWDNet and TextCNN. Finally, we compared the performance of all detection methods on the test sets. The experimental results for the 1166 datasets have been presented in Table \ref{1166-dataset}, while the ROC curves for SWDNet and the TextCNN model are shown in Fig.~\ref{fig12}.

Due to the significant decrease in the number of samples obtained from small web shells after conversion to opcode sequences and deduplication, we expanded our dataset to investigate the impact of dataset size on experimental results. To accomplish this, we duplicated the small web shell data, sequentially numbered them up to 1166, and generated an additional 583 benign files and 583 corresponding stealth web shells. These data sets were constructed following the standard procedures outlined in Chapter 3, involving the search for new data, opcode conversion, deduplication, manual analysis, and numbering. As a result, we obtained three experimental data sets, each consisting of 1166 samples.

The data sets were then divided into training and testing sets, with an 8:2 ratio, to respectively train the SWDNet and TextCNN models. Finally, we compared the performance of all detection methods on the testing sets. The experimental results for the 1166 data sets are presented in Table 1, while the ROC curves for SWDNet and the TextCNN model are shown in Figure 2.

Since the number of samples obtained from small web shells significantly decreased after converting them into opcode sequences and deduplicating, we expanded our dataset to examine the impact of dataset size on experimental results. To achieve this, we replicated the small web shell data, sequentially numbered them up to 1166, and generated an additional 583 benign files and 583 corresponding stealth web shells. These datasets were constructed following the standard procedures outlined in Chapter 3, which included searching for new data, opcode conversion, deduplication, manual analysis, and numbering. As a result, we obtained three experimental datasets, each containing 1166 samples.

The datasets were then divided into training and testing sets with an 8:2 ratio, and used to separately train the SWDNet and TextCNN models. Finally, we compared the performance of all detection methods on the testing sets. The experimental results for the 1166 datasets are presented in Table 1, while the ROC curves for SWDNet and the TextCNN model are shown in Figure 2.

帮我缩减以下英文学术论文：
To begin with, we conducted a horizontal comparison and analysis on two sets of experimental data to evaluate the impact of SWDNet. Overall, the performance on the 1166 dataset was better than that on the 583 dataset, despite the presence of some duplicated small web shell in the dataset. Nevertheless, the effect of dataset size was observed to outweigh that of repeated data. SWDNet was originally designed to compare the distance between small web shell and stealth web shells, as well as the distance between benign files and stealth web shells, based on similarity comparison using distance calculation rather than feature comparison. Therefore, the reuse of small web shell only once did not have a significant impact on the experimental results. Conversely, an increase in the amount of data improved the model's performance.

Next, we performed a vertical comparison of SWDNet method against other methods. The results showed that SWDNet achieved the highest accuracy and recall rate, indicating its effectiveness in detecting stealth web shells. In contrast, other methods did not consider stealth web shells, resulting in poor performance and lower precision. Although SWDNet had a slightly lower precision rate compared to other methods because they performed better in identifying benign files, it also leaded to increased precision rate. It's possible that SWDNet may not completely distinguish between benign files and stealth web shells, both of which have highly similar code, leading to suboptimal performance in identifying benign files. Nonetheless, TextCNN demonstrated the advantages of deep learning detection models by achieving the best precision rate, with larger datasets contributing to improving its performance. Combining recall and precision, SWDNet achieved the highest F1-score. In summary, SWDNet exhibited superior detection performance for small web shell, stealth web shells, and benign files.

Furthermore, SWDNet exhibited strong robustness in detecting stealth web shells, irrespective of the variant of small web shell employed, owing to the use of opcode sequence features rather than source codes. Moreover, the location of the small web shell insertion was not predetermined and was randomized during the construction of stealth web shells, ensuring that its placement did not compromise detection capabilities. Overall, SWDNet guarantees reliable detection of stealth web shells, which makes it an effective tool in identifying and preventing cyber attacks.

We conducted a comparison and analysis on two sets of experimental data to evaluate the impact of SWDNet. The performance on the larger 1166 dataset was better than that on the smaller 583 dataset, even though there were some duplicated small web shells in the dataset. The effect of dataset size was found to be more significant than the presence of repeated data. SWDNet, designed to compare the distances between small web shells and stealth web shells, as well as between benign files and stealth web shells, based on similarity comparison using distance calculation, showed consistent performance despite the reuse of small web shells. The increase in the amount of data improved the model's performance.

We also conducted a comparison of the SWDNet method with other methods. SWDNet achieved the highest accuracy and recall rate, indicating its effectiveness in detecting stealth web shells. Other methods that did not consider stealth web shells showed poor performance and lower precision. While SWDNet had a slightly lower precision rate compared to other methods in identifying benign files, it contributed to a higher precision rate overall. The similarity between benign files and stealth web shells, which have highly similar code, may have impacted the performance in distinguishing between the two. However, TextCNN demonstrated the advantages of deep learning detection models by achieving the best precision rate, with larger datasets improving its performance. Combining recall and precision, SWDNet achieved the highest F1-score. Overall, SWDNet exhibited superior detection performance for small web shells, stealth web shells, and benign files.

Furthermore, SWDNet exhibited robustness in detecting different variants of small web shells by utilizing opcode sequence features instead of source codes. The random placement of small web shells during the construction of stealth web shells ensured that the detection capabilities were not compromised. In summary, SWDNet provides reliable detection of stealth web shells, making it an effective tool for identifying and preventing cyber attacks.

帮我缩减以下英文学术论文：
In order to evaluate the performance of SWDNet, we first explored the influence of the model's parameters. Subsequently, we trained a TextCNN detection model separately as a benchmark and measured its capacity to detect stealth web shells. Additionally, we also applied several commonly used web shell detection tools to test their effectiveness in detecting stealth web shells.

To evaluate the performance of SWDNet, we examined the impact of its parameters and trained a separate TextCNN detection model as a benchmark. We also tested the effectiveness of several commonly-used web shell detection tools in detecting stealth web shells.
